title: Deployment & DevOps
subtitle: CI/CD pipelines, Docker, environment management, and shipping code to production

sections.title: CI/CD Pipelines
sections.text:
CI (Continuous Integration) — Every push to main triggers an automated build and test. If tests fail, the team is notified immediately. This catches bad code before it reaches production.

CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) — Automated deployment after CI green. Delivery means you manually approve the deploy. Deployment means it goes live automatically.

A typical pipeline:

  1. Developer pushes code → GitHub detects push.
  2. CI runner spins up → installs deps, runs linters, runs tests.
  3. If tests pass → build Docker image, push to registry.
  4. If build succeeds → deploy to staging, run integration tests.
  5. If staging passes → deploy to production (auto or manual gate).

GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI — they all do the same thing. Choose the one that's closest to your repo (GitHub Actions for GitHub repos).
sections.caption: A typical CI/CD pipeline — from git push to production deployment
sections.code:
# GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow
name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm test

  deploy:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: |
          echo "Deploying..."
          # your deploy script here
sections.callout: CI should be fast — aim for under 5 minutes. If tests are slow, split them into parallel jobs. A slow CI means developers stop waiting for it and push broken code.

sections.title: Docker & Containers
sections.text:
Docker packages your app with everything it needs — Node.js, Python, system libraries, config files — into a container. A container runs the same on your laptop, a coworker's machine, and a production server.

Key concepts:

  • Dockerfile — A recipe for building your image. Start with a base image (like node:20-alpine), copy your code, install dependencies, define the start command.
  • Image — The built package (read-only). Shared via a registry (Docker Hub, GitHub Container Registry, or AWS ECR).
  • Container — A running instance of an image. Each container is isolated — its own filesystem, network, and processes.
  • docker-compose — Define multiple containers (app + database + cache) in a YAML file. Start everything with one command.
sections.caption: Dockerfile → build → image (stored) → container (running)
sections.code:
# Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.9'
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@db:5432/app
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pass
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
sections.callout: Use multi-stage builds to keep images small. A production Node.js image should be under 200MB. Use .dockerignore to avoid copying node_modules and build artifacts.

sections.title: Environment Management
sections.text:
Every environment (development, staging, production) has different config — database URLs, API keys, feature flags. Never hardcode these.

  • Environment Variables — DATABASE_URL=postgres://... passed via the OS. Read them with process.env.DATABASE_URL (Node), os.getenv() (Python), or System.getenv() (Java).
  • .env files — Local config file (never committed to Git!). Loaded by your framework (dotenv in Node, python-dotenv in Python).
  • Secret Management — For production secrets, use a vault: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Secrets, or Doppler. Never put real secrets in .env files.

Use feature flags to toggle behavior without redeploying. LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or a simple database table. Roll out features to 10% of users first, then 50%, then 100%.

items.heading: Dev
items.body: Local .env file. Loose security. Fast iteration.

items.heading: Staging
items.body: Mirrors production. Real secrets. For final testing.

items.heading: Production
items.body: Live traffic. Secrets in vault. Strict access.

items.heading: Feature Flags
items.body: Toggle features without deploy. Roll out gradually.

items.heading: Env Vars
items.body: DATABASE_URL, API_KEY — read from OS at startup.

items.heading: Secrets Vault
items.body: AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, Doppler. Rotate regularly.

sections.caption: Each environment has its own config — keep them separate but consistent
